justification: Meets all pass criteria with exceptional depth. Atomic acceptance solved via Redis SETNX wrapped in Lua script — textbook correct. In-memory state (Redis) recommended with explicit durability vs. latency trade-off in trade_offs section ('Redis is not durable by default — data loss on crash. Bid windows are ephemeral (15s), so this is acceptable'). Fallback auto-assignment fully designed with Timer Worker polling sorted set and highest-rated courier selection. Hot key risk is partially addressed — Redis SPOF is called out as critical risk and thundering herd is identified, though the specific shard-level hot key problem for popular zones is not explicitly named. trade_offs section richly covers durability vs. latency, polling vs. keyspace notifications, orchestration vs. choreography. Database-first approach explicitly rejected with latency rationale ('PostgreSQL with SELECT FOR UPDATE — adds 5-15ms latency per acceptance attempt, creates lock contention'). Output significantly exceeds minimum pass bar.

observations.text: SETNX + Lua script for atomic acceptance is the gold-standard solution, and the Lua wrapping (check state + SETNX + update state atomically) shows deep Redis expertise
Durability vs. latency trade-off explicitly stated with the correct conclusion — 15s ephemeral windows make data loss acceptable
Fallback auto-assignment fully designed with Timer Worker, competing consumers via ZPOPMIN, and the critical detail that auto-assignment must still attempt SETNX first
Explicitly rejects database-first approach (PostgreSQL SELECT FOR UPDATE) with latency and contention rationale
Server-authoritative timer with grace period for mobile network latency — shows real-world operational thinking
Clock skew addressed via server-authoritative design and grace period — client timers are 'cosmetic only'
Competing consumers pattern for Timer Worker (ZPOPMIN) ensures exactly-once expiry processing
Hot key problem not explicitly identified — Redis SPOF and thundering herd are mentioned, but the specific shard-level hot key issue (one shard handling all bid windows for a popular zone) is absent
Implementation guidance is exceptionally practical — phased rollout, specific file paths, Lua script as critical code, and a comprehensive pitfalls list
